summary: The concept is directionally reasonable—fail closed on ambiguity, require human confirmation, and preserve the scope gate—but the current design cannot yet support its claimed confidence. Tier 2 lacks task-level analogy data, historical successes are confounded by the heavyweight pipeline being removed, path-based risk cannot be known reliably before localization, and post-hoc escalation has no compatible state-machine path. The fast-path contract also conflicts with the required audit fields and exact recon format. Treat this as a promising hypothesis requiring a smaller, explicitly stateful experiment rather than a ready scoring architecture.

findings.claim: Tier 2 cannot compute the proposed “close historical analogy” from the repository’s actual telemetry.
findings.evidence: `worker-performance.jsonl` contains only aggregate `(backend,type)` rows. Its source, `task-outcomes.jsonl`, records only `run_id`, generic task type, backend/model, status, blocked, and rework rounds—no request text, touched paths, semantic category, impact, diff size, review failures, or similarity key. `compound-v-scorecard.py` explicitly aggregates by `(backend,type)`. A healthy `bounded_crud` cell therefore cannot establish that a proposed CSS edit resembles prior CSS edits.
findings.recommendation: Either remove Tier 2 from v1 or introduce a separate task-level triage-outcome schema containing predicted and actual paths, category, diff characteristics, sensitive-surface verdict, escalation reason, review/test result, and taxonomy version. Require an explicit minimum sample count and deterministic similarity rule.

findings.claim: Even enriched historical outcomes would initially be counterfactual and selection-biased: success under the full pipeline is not evidence that the same task succeeds safely without that pipeline.
findings.evidence: All current `task-outcomes.jsonl` entries are successful jobs produced after pre-flights, partition validation, scope gating, and mandatory review. The proposed fast path removes some of those interventions, so low rework under the old treatment may have been caused by the very checks being skipped. Current data also contains only Claude/Opus executions, further limiting transfer to another fast-path configuration.
findings.recommendation: Do not let legacy outcomes lower ceremony. Initially use history only to escalate. Collect shadow-mode predictions while still running the full pipeline, then run a bounded prospective trial and measure false-low rate before enabling historical evidence to recommend a fast path.

findings.claim: Tier 1 can confidently classify a request as low before it knows the actual path or semantic fan-out, and the claimed pre-existing sensitive-path list does not exist in the cited routing policy.
findings.evidence: `routing-policy.md` defines semantic sensitive classes—security, auth, payments, PII, and a11y—after job scope is understood; it does not provide a path-pattern list covering billing/session. Before archaeology, “make button X red” may resolve to a global design token, shared theme file, generated stylesheet, contrast/a11y state, or branded compliance surface. Having an LLM map request prose into a path-derived bucket reintroduces the uncertain inference the static taxonomy was meant to avoid.
findings.recommendation: Insert a bounded read-only localization step before any low verdict: resolve exact candidate files, selector/token fan-out, generated/shared-file status, and sensitive semantics. Permit low only through a high-precision allowlist; unknown paths, shared tokens, a11y states, or semantic/path disagreement must select the full pipeline.

findings.claim: Post-hoc diff re-verification is not sufficient because it occurs after the checks whose preventive value was skipped, and the current state machine has no legal escalation transition.
findings.evidence: `state-machine.md` is linear: `SPEC_READY → PREFLIGHT_DONE → PARTITION_VERIFIED → DISPATCHED → COLLECTED → REVIEWED → MERGED`. There is no fast-path state or transition from dispatch back to pre-flight/planning. `compound-v-scope-check.py` proves only that writes stayed within allowed paths; it cannot detect that an allowed generic file changes auth, accessibility, generated output, or broad runtime behavior. In direct mode, `execution-manifest.md` also documents that modifications to pre-existing untracked/ignored files are unattributable.
findings.recommendation: Run fast-path work in a quarantined worktree. Define explicit `FASTPATH_DISPATCHED` and `ESCALATION_REQUIRED` states. If actual-diff classification fails, do not continue with the existing patch: preserve it for evidence, run the missing pre-flights and planning stages, then explicitly revalidate or reimplement before merge.

findings.claim: The proposed skip behavior is incompatible with the current manifest and review contracts, so the fast path is not executable as described.
findings.evidence: `execution-manifest.md` requires `audits: {archaeology, domain, library}` and auto-includes all three audit files in job reads. Skipped pre-flights do not currently define sentinel artifacts. Moreover, Phase 1C skips only pure prose/UX copy with zero technical surface; a CSS/framework color edit does not clearly qualify, contrary to the plan’s statement that existing 1A/1C skips cover the example. The plan also never states which of the mandatory three review passes the fast path skips or retains.
findings.recommendation: Specify a complete fast-path contract: sentinel audit values or validated skip records, corresponding manifest-schema changes, state transitions, exact review-pass matrix, and which tests remain mandatory. Add a validator fixture for the one-file CSS example.

findings.claim: Putting the triage score inside the recon document overloads an artifact whose existing contract and consumers have different semantics.
findings.evidence: `phase-0-recon.md` requires exactly five ordered sections, no YAML front matter, and describes recon as evidence that must not select an approach or routing decision. It also produces no document when plumbing, KB, config-off, decline, or no-engine gates stop the run. A fast-path score is control-plane state needed even in those cases, and exposing “low impact” inside the document read by brainstorming and 1B/1C creates an anchoring signal.
findings.recommendation: Store pre-evaluation as a separate machine-readable record linked from the recon and manifest, with evidence provenance, taxonomy version, decision, human response, and later actual-diff verdict. Keep the recon’s five-section evidence contract unchanged.

findings.claim: The scoring and offer rules are internally under-specified and cannot be tested deterministically.
findings.evidence: Tier 3 is said to run only when neither Tier 1 nor Tier 2 signals, yet the final result is described as a weighted combination of whichever tiers signal. No weights, band boundaries, similarity cutoff, minimum sample count, disagreement rule, confidence definition, size threshold, or axis-specific low threshold is declared. “Always human-confirmed” also conflicts with reusing an `ask/auto/off` convention unless `auto` is newly redefined.
findings.recommendation: Replace prose scoring with a truth table. Define every input, threshold, missing-data behavior, conflict behavior, taxonomy version, and displayed evidence. Use `ask|off` for fast-path selection if confirmation is mandatory, and combine the research and fast-path questions into one bounded interaction.

findings.claim: A single-job partition is vacuously disjoint but can still have an unsafe or meaningless write scope.
findings.evidence: The partition exception proves only that there is no sibling collision. It does not prove that `write_allowed` is a single exact file rather than a broad glob such as `src/**`, nor that the predicted file is not a lockfile, generated artifact, registry, barrel, migration, or shared token. Without archaeology, exact ownership may remain guessed, violating `execution-manifest.md`’s “unclear scope never dispatches” rule.
findings.recommendation: Allow the exception only for one literal normalized path, confirmed by the localization step, with no glob metacharacters and no shared/generated/config/migration category. Otherwise require normal partition review.

findings.claim: Raw git churn, revert density, and fix-keyword density are unsuitable as evidence for a low-risk verdict and can create misleading hot or cold paths.
findings.evidence: Mass formatting, vendored/generated code, squash merges, renames, uneven commit conventions, and frequently improved high-value modules distort these measures; low churn can simply mean untested or rarely maintained code. Research supports normalized relative churn, while absolute churn is reported as a poor predictor in the [Microsoft Research study](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/use-of-relative-code-churn-measures-to-predict-system-defect-density/). Commit-message keyword labeling also depends heavily on repository discipline.
findings.recommendation: Use history only as a conservative escalation signal. Normalize by component size, age, and exposure; exclude generated/vendor/merge/format commits; require minimum history and issue/revert linkage where available; never interpret absent or low churn as evidence of safety.

findings.claim: Automatically generating ADR, C4, and SAD artifacts for V-memory is not justified by current evidence and risks context bloat and stale duplicated truth.
findings.evidence: The repository already generates citation-verified `architecture.md`, `business-logic.md`, and `tech-context.md`. A 2026 review found ADR and C4 each appeared in only one study and that 74% of surveyed GenAI-architecture work used no named modeling language, so evidence for these formats specifically is sparse ([Journal of Systems and Software review](https://trepo.tuni.fi/bitstream/10024/231421/1/Generative_AI_for_software_architecture._Applications_challenges_and_future_directions.pdf)). A 2026 context-file study found unnecessary generated context can reduce agent success and raise inference cost ([Evaluating AGENTS.md](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988)). Repository exploration quality matters, but that supports measured retrieval quality rather than a particular document format ([SWE-Explore](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07297)).
findings.recommendation: Do not generate SADs. Add concise, human-approved ADRs only for genuine decisions with alternatives and consequences. Offer text/DSL C4 views only for complex multi-service topology, with cited sources and staleness tracking. A/B-test retrieval relevance and downstream task success before expanding onboarding.

blind_spots_checked: Partition and glob expansion: checked the single-job exception against manifest disjointness, exact-scope, shared-resource, and unclear-scope rules.
Coupling: checked recon consumers, audit-path requirements, Phase 1 skip rules, manifest materialization, and review-gate dependencies.
Scope enforcement: checked tracked, untracked, ignored, rename, symlink, worktree, and documented direct-mode blind spots in the real scope gate.
Model routing: no independent backend/worktree violation was found in the proposal; Codex isolation remains governed by the existing manifest invariant.
Acceptance: checked score composition, confidence, thresholds, sample sufficiency, user-offer semantics, and executable validator coverage.
Sequencing and resume: checked the proposal against the current linear run-state machine and escalation behavior.
Risk: checked false-low taxonomy cases, historical-data confounding, feedback/selection bias, and post-hoc detection limits.
Bonus extensions: checked onboarding’s current write surface and current evidence for churn metrics, ADR, C4, SAD, generated context, and repository exploration.
